content: Hello. I was wondering if I could briefly ask for some clarification on the current state of Rust for Linux kernel module development? I am trying to make a simple Rust firewall kernel module that hooks net filter as a learning exercise (I have a working version in C). My confusion comes from the documentation here https://rust-for-linux.github.io/docs/kernel/net/filter/struct.Registration.html because it seems straight forward, however, my 6.8 ubuntu kernel doesn't seem to have anything like this (I did get a hello world km working in Rust because the kernel::prelude module does seem to exist unlike kernel::net::filter). Looking at more recent kernel versions on the linux and rust-for-linux github pages shows something similar where the net . rs file used as a source in the documentation here https://rust-for-linux.github.io/docs/src/kernel/net.rs.html#3-392 doesn't match either https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/blob/rust-next/rust/kernel/net.rs or https://github.com/torvalds/linux/blob/master/rust/kernel/net.rs. I didn't see much looking at the file's history either. I am still pretty new to this sort of development, so I apologize if I have missed something obvious. Does anyone have advice on other resources or strategies I could use to double check if creating such a kernel module is even possible? I apologize if this is the wrong way to ask this question, please let me know if I need to move it or delete it.

No worries, it is confusing.

The docs you are looking at are the ones from the out-of-tree days, which we have kept for reference, please see https://rust-for-linux.com/branches.

The current documentation for the next cycle is at https://rust.docs.kernel.org/next/ (please see the side bar at https://rust-for-linux.com for more links).

At some point we want to delete or replace the old ones with a redirect to avoid confusion (they are not in the sidebar of the main page anymore).

The networking abstractions that you used are in the old rust branch. As Miguel already said, those are from when RfL was still out of tree.

New kernel code can only be accepted if there exists a user (ie a driver). Nobody has yet written a network driver in Rust, so no abstractions exist.

We are aware that this is a bit of a chicken and egg problem (no driver -> no abstractions -> hard to write a driver...).

You said that you wanted to try it as a learning experience, so I guess you don't want the driver to be upstreamed, right?

Yes, I am just trying to learn a little more about Rust and the Linux kernel at the same time - I have no delusions that I'll be able to make anything upstreamable! So to confirm my understanding, I am trying to write an out-of-tree module (as it is the simplest concept for me to understand and quick to test) which is probably not feasible with the current branch unless I create my own Rust wrapper for the C netfilter framework (or re-implement netfilter using system calls, ect?), add it to net . rs and recompile a new kernel (which could then use that abstraction to do network out of tree modules)? Thank you so much for the help, I really appreciate the time and effort taken to help a noob!!!! I think I'm going to need to take a step back to study the Linux kernel from scratch a bit and then look at how rust drivers are being written plus just learn more about Rust. Thank you!!!!
